title: Variables: val & var
description: Understand Kotlin's two variable keywords: val (immutable) and var (mutable). Learn how type inference works and why Kotlin favors immutability.

concept.definition: val declares a read-only binding — you can't reassign it after initialization. var declares a mutable variable you can reassign anytime. Kotlin infers types so you rarely need to write them explicitly.
concept.analogy: val is like a sealed envelope — once you put a value in, you can't replace it. var is a regular folder — you can swap the content whenever you want.
keyPoints: val = read-only (not reassignable)
var = mutable (can be reassigned)
Type is inferred from the initial value
Prefer val by default; use var only when mutation is needed

title: Data Types
description: Explore Kotlin's core types: Int, Long, Double, Boolean, Char, String, and how string templates make formatting easy.

concept.definition: Kotlin has a rich set of built-in types: Int (32-bit integer), Long (64-bit), Double (64-bit float), Boolean (true/false), Char (single character), and String. String templates (${expr}) allow embedding expressions directly in strings.
concept.analogy: Types are like labelled containers — an Int container only holds whole numbers, a Double container holds decimals, and a String container holds text.
keyPoints: Int, Long, Double, Float cover numbers
Boolean is true/false
Char is a single character in single quotes
String templates: "Hello, $name" or "${name.length} chars"

title: Functions
description: Write functions with fun, use default parameter values, call with named arguments, and use expression-body functions for concise one-liners.

concept.definition: Kotlin functions start with fun. Parameters can have default values, making them optional at the call site. Named arguments let callers specify which parameter they're passing, improving readability. A function with a single expression can use = instead of a block body.
concept.analogy: A function is a recipe. Default parameters are like saying 'use salt unless the cook specifies otherwise'. Named arguments are like ordering 'with extra cheese, no onions' — you name what you're changing.
keyPoints: fun name(param: Type): ReturnType { ... }
Default values: fun greet(name: String = "World")
Named args: greet(name = "Alice")
Expression body: fun double(x: Int) = x * 2

title: Control Flow: if & when
description: Use if as an expression that returns a value, and when as a powerful alternative to switch/match that handles ranges, types, and arbitrary conditions.

concept.definition: In Kotlin, if is an expression — it returns a value, so you can use it on the right side of an assignment. when is Kotlin's switch on steroids: branches can match constants, ranges, type checks, or boolean conditions.
concept.analogy: if-as-expression is like a vending machine — you put in a condition and get a value back. when is like a sorting office that can route packages by weight, shape, or label — all in one block.
keyPoints: val x = if (a > b) a else b — if returns a value
when replaces if-else chains and switch statements
when branches: constant, in range, is Type, else
when can be used without an argument as a cleaner if-else chain

title: Loops: for, while, repeat
description: Iterate with for over ranges and collections, use while for condition-based loops, and repeat for simple counted repetition.

concept.definition: Kotlin provides for (range/collection iteration), while (condition-checked before each iteration), do-while (condition-checked after), and repeat(n) (runs a lambda n times). Ranges are created with .. (inclusive) or until (exclusive) and can have a step.
concept.analogy: for with a range is like walking numbered steps on a staircase. repeat is like setting a timer for N chimes. while is like waiting at a door — keep waiting as long as it's closed.
keyPoints: for (i in 1..5) — inclusive range 1 to 5
for (i in 0 until 5) — exclusive: 0,1,2,3,4
for (i in 10 downTo 1 step 2) — countdown
repeat(3) { ... } — simplest counted loop

title: Null Safety
description: Understand Kotlin's type system that distinguishes nullable (String?) from non-null (String) types. Use safe call (?.), Elvis operator (?:), and non-null assertion (!!).

concept.definition: In Kotlin, regular types (String, Int) can never be null. To allow null you add ?: String?, Int?. Safe call (?.) accesses a member only if the object is non-null. Elvis (?:) provides a fallback value when something is null. !! asserts non-null and throws if it's null.
concept.analogy: Think of ? as a safety label: 'this box might be empty'. Safe call is like checking if the box has anything before opening it. Elvis (?:) is saying 'use the value if present, or use this backup instead'.
keyPoints: String — never null; String? — can be null
val len = text?.length — returns null if text is null
val len = text?.length ?: 0 — fallback to 0
!! asserts non-null — avoid unless you're certain

title: Classes & Properties
description: Define classes with primary constructors, declare properties directly in the constructor, add member functions, and understand the difference between val and var properties.

concept.definition: Kotlin classes can declare properties right in the primary constructor. Methods are functions inside the class body. By default classes are final (not inheritable) — use open to allow subclassing. Properties with val generate a getter; var generates getter and setter.
concept.analogy: A class is a blueprint for creating objects. The primary constructor is the form you fill out when creating a new one — each field in the form becomes a property of every instance.
keyPoints: class Person(val name: String, var age: Int)
Properties in constructor = automatic get/set
Methods defined with fun inside the class body
Classes are final by default — add open to allow inheritance

title: Data Classes
description: Use data class to get equals, hashCode, toString, and copy for free. Learn destructuring declarations to unpack data class instances.

concept.definition: A data class is a class whose purpose is to hold data. Kotlin auto-generates equals(), hashCode(), toString(), and copy(). copy() lets you create a new instance with some properties changed. You can also destructure a data class: val (a, b) = point.
concept.analogy: A data class is like a named tuple with superpowers — Kotlin writes all the boilerplate (equals, toString, copy) so you don't have to.
keyPoints: data class Point(val x: Int, val y: Int)
toString() auto: "Point(x=1, y=2)"
copy(): val p2 = p.copy(y = 5)
Destructuring: val (x, y) = point

defaultCode:
data class Point(val x: Int, val y: Int)

fun main() {
    val p = Point(1, 2)
    println(p)
    val p2 = p.copy(y = 5)
    println(p2)
    val (x, y) = p2
    println("x=$x y=$y")
}

title: Collections: List, Map, Set
description: Create read-only and mutable lists, maps, and sets. Use listOf, mutableListOf, mapOf, and common operations like filter, map, and forEach.

concept.definition: Kotlin separates read-only collections (listOf, mapOf, setOf) from mutable ones (mutableListOf, mutableMapOf). Read-only doesn't mean the data is immutable — it means this reference doesn't expose mutation operations. Common operations: filter, map, forEach, find, any, all.
concept.analogy: A read-only list is like a printed menu — you can read it, but you can't add items to it. A mutable list is a whiteboard menu — you can add and erase items.
keyPoints: listOf(1,2,3) — read-only list
mutableListOf(1,2,3) — add/remove allowed
mapOf("a" to 1) — read-only map with to infix
filter, map, forEach work on all collections

title: Lambdas & Higher-Order Functions
description: Write lambdas as function values, pass them to higher-order functions, use it for single-parameter lambdas, and understand trailing lambda syntax.

concept.definition: A lambda is an anonymous function: { x: Int -> x * 2 }. Higher-order functions accept or return functions. Kotlin's standard library is full of them: forEach, filter, map, let, run, apply. When a lambda is the last argument, it can be placed outside the parentheses (trailing lambda syntax).
concept.analogy: A lambda is like a sticky note with instructions — you can hand it to someone else to execute later. Higher-order functions are people who accept those sticky notes and act on them.
keyPoints: val double = { x: Int -> x * 2 }
it refers to the single parameter of a lambda
Trailing lambda: list.forEach { println(it) }
Function type: (Int) -> Int

title: Extension Functions
description: Add new functions to existing classes without modifying them or using inheritance. Understand receiver types and how extensions are resolved at compile time.

concept.definition: An extension function lets you add a function to any class — even classes you don't own (like String or Int) — without subclassing. Inside the extension, this refers to the receiver object. They are resolved statically (at compile time), not polymorphically.
concept.analogy: Extension functions are like clipping a custom tool to someone else's toolbox — the toolbox owner didn't design it, but you can use it as if they did.
keyPoints: fun String.shout() = this.uppercase() + "!"
this inside an extension = the receiver instance
Extensions don't modify the original class
Cannot access private members of the receiver

title: Coroutines Basics
description: Understand coroutines as lightweight concurrent units. Use runBlocking, launch, and suspend functions to write sequential-looking async code.

concept.definition: A coroutine is a suspendable computation — it can pause (suspend) without blocking the underlying thread, then resume later. launch starts a coroutine that runs concurrently. suspend functions can only be called from a coroutine or another suspend function. runBlocking bridges regular code and coroutines.
concept.analogy: A coroutine is like a task on a to-do list that you can pause and pick up later — unlike threads which occupy a worker completely, coroutines share workers efficiently.
keyPoints: suspend fun — can be paused without blocking a thread
launch { } — starts a coroutine (fire-and-forget)
runBlocking { } — bridges blocking world to coroutines
delay(ms) — suspends without blocking the thread
